YLrRxUUHl44.full_text: What is the best options trading strategy for beginners? Well, it's definitely not chasing overpriced calls and puts on hot stocks where your chances of winning the trade are far worse than you actually think. In fact, that's a great way to lose money trading options. But we'd rather find a winning strategy that involves following a simple set of criteria for trade entry and a simple and repeatable set of rules to implement that strategy. then and only then can it be considered to be a solid strategy that's profitable and suitable for traders of all levels. I've made hundreds of videos on strategies that you can learn and apply to your own trading. And today I want to challenge you to follow this step-by-step strategy lesson closely. And if you do that, if you stay with me on this, by the end of the video, you will have learned one of the most solid and respected options trading strategies out there, the double diagonal option strategy. that is traded by professional options traders all over the world. Now, in this video, we're going to be learning three key criteria for you to use as your strategy checklist. Then, we're going to teach you the easy repeatable process for how to implement the trade once your criteria are met. And then, we'll show you a full 12-month example of how this strategy performed last year, so you can see how powerful it is. So, let's go ahead and dive in. >> [music] >> yeah. >> So the first step in this strategy process is to find a stock, an ETF or an index which has a very flat price chart. So let's for example take TLT, the EyesShares long treasury bond ETF that holds 20 to 30year Treasury bonds. And so let's say that on the first day of January in 2025, we had looked at this chart and we would have noticed that the index had actually been trading at a pretty tight range for the last few years, hitting as low as 82 and as high as 109, but spending most of its time in that 87 to 100 range for the previous two years, closing at 8757 on the first trading day of January. And so we'd call this a pretty flat chart. we can move on to step two, which is to check as to whether this options chain has at least monthly expirations. You see, most stocks, ETFs, and indexes actually have options chains expiring weekly, but on the other hand, some options chains only have quarterly options, and that's not going to work for this strategy. So, pull up your online broker platform like we're showing you here and just make sure that there is at least one expiration in each month of the upcoming year. And as you can see, TLT does in fact have at least one expiration in every month of the upcoming year. So, we can check that off the list. And then the final prerequisite is step three in which we ensure that the individual strike prices have tight bid spreads which basically means that you'll be able to get a fair and competitive price for the options that you'll be trading. And so looking at this options chain of TLT, we can see that the bid ass spreads are very tight, usually one to three cents in width. And that's a very tight liquid options chain and perfect for this strategy. So, at this point, we've checked off all three boxes, and we're ready to go because TLT meets all of our criteria. Let's head back to the third Friday in January 2025, which was January 17th. And incidentally, we picked the third Friday of the month because options chains that have monthly options will always have an option chain expiring on the third Friday of the month. So, it's a very convenient day to start trades where you'll be in the trade for about a month. And this strategy that we're sharing with you today is traded 12 times a year, once a month, starting on the third Friday of the month. So, as you can see on that day, the stock had closed at 8719. And so, we're going to pull up an options chain expiring on the third Friday of that next month, February 21st. And as you can see, there's a column on that options chain called delta. And as you move up the options chain, you'll see the first option you hit that has a delta below 20 is the 91 strike price, which has a delta of 18.23, as you can see. And then if you move down the put side on that delta column, you'll see the first option that has a delta below 20 on the put side is that 83 put. So, we're going to go ahead and sell 10 each of both the 91 calls and the 83 puts. Before we go any further, you might be wondering why we picked those below 20 delta call and put options to sell. And that's very important. So, we're going to take a minute on this before we go on. You see, the delta of an option is a mathematically arrived at prediction of how much an options price is likely to move based upon how much the options index itself moves. And while it's a little bit of an involved topic, suffice it to say that an options delta has also been found to be highly correlated to the probability that an option will expire with value on the day that it expires. And so, for instance, in the case of a 20 delta call option, there's approximately a 20% chance that the stock will close above that call option strike price on the day that the call expires. And so if you think about it, it also means that there's an 80% chance of the stock closing below that call strike price on expiration day. And when does a call option have value in expiration? That happens only if the stock closes above that call strike price on the day it expires. Because at that point, you'd have the right to own the shares at a price cheaper than you get them in the open market. So that option has a lot of value obviously. And the exact same thing is true for the 20 delta put except to the downside. And so the options market is saying that there's a 20% chance that the stock will close below the put strike price or in this case 83 and therefore an 80% chance that it won't expire below that price. And [snorts] so what we're trying to do here is to sell options all of which have less of a likelihood of expiring with value than with no value. Now, why would that be a goal of ours? Why do we want to sell options and then have them go to zero? Well, as you'll find out in a minute, that is actually an awesome outcome, even though it doesn't sound like that initially. So, stick with me and you'll see. The second step before we launch the trade is to head over to the options chain, which expires about two months out. in this case, March 21st, uh, about two months later. And we're going to go ahead and buy 10 of those 96 calls, five points higher than the 91 calls we sold. And we're also going to go down to the 78 puts and buy 10 of those, five points lower than the 83 puts that we sold. And so, we've sold two sets of options in the February options chain. And we bought two sets of options in the March options chain. And so when we do this, selling calls and puts above and below where the stock is trading and buying farther out calls and puts at an equal distance from the options we sold. When we do that, we have created what options traders refer to as a double diagonal options trade, which is a great strategy for trading flat price charts like we found with TLT. Now, before we take a look at the trade outcome, let's first dig into the positive cash flow we received when we first entered the double diagonal. Starting with the 91 calls that we sold, we received a price of 36 cents for those. But remember, each option uh represents 100 shares of stock. So, we multiply that by 100. And since we sold 10 of them, when you multiply it all together, we ended up receiving $360 for the 91 calls. Using that same kind of math, we paid $240 for the 96 calls that we bought, those March calls, and we received $340 for the 83 puts we sold. And finally, we paid 220 for the 78 puts that we bought. And netting it all down, we received $240 of cash from this double diagonal trade. And to enter this particular trade, please note that your broker is going to require you to have $4,760 in your account to make this trade, which is also the trade's worst case scenario. Let's move to the expiration day of this trade, which is February 21st. And as you can see, TLT closed at 8961 that day. Well, if you'll recall, we sold the 91 calls and the 83 puts. Both of those expired that day while the long options in March are still alive. Since the 91 calls are above where the stock closed and the 83 puts are below where the stock closed, both options actually expire worthless because puts only have value if the stock closes below their strike price and calls only have value if the stock closes above their strike price. And if you think about it, we shouldn't be too surprised about this because both the calls and the puts had less than a 20% chance of expiring with any value in the first place. This is because we picked options with deltas lower than 20 as we explained earlier. So both of those options expire worthless. But, and this is key to understand, the 96 calls we bought in March and the 78 puts we bought in March both have value still. Why? Well, those have 28 more days before they expire. So, there's a possibility that TLT will rally up to 96 or sell off down to 78 and those options have to retain some value because that risk is still a possibility. And the one selling those options has to get paid for that risk. And as you can see, the 96 calls are worth 11 cents and the 78 calls are worth about 1 cent. So when we close the trade, we're going to get some value from those. And so when we calculate the profit from this trade, we'll start with that initial $240 that we received when we first entered the trade, then we'll acknowledge that both the 91 calls and the 83 puts that we sold in February, they both expired worthless as we talked about. And if on the day those expire, we also sold the two remaining long options that expire in March, the 96 calls and the 78 puts. As you can see, we received $110 for the 96 calls. And we received $10 for the 78 puts, resulting in a final trade profit of $360. It's beginning to make a little bit of sense that we would have picked a price chart of a stock that wasn't moving a lot because then if we kind of surround it with short calls and short puts and the stock's price stays inside of a reasonable range, then there's a good likelihood that both the short calls placed above the market and the short puts placed below the market will both expire worthless and we'll still be able to collect cash for selling off the long options resulting in an overall profit. And that's the basic concept behind a double diagonal. So with systems like this, a best practice is to repeat the trade month after month as these are annual campaigns and you like to look at the profit and loss from all the trades in the campaign on an annual basis to get a sense of the edge of the strategy. So let's move to the next trade which will start immediately at the expiration of the previous trade. And so in this case, we'll be selling the 93 calls with a delta of 1836 and the 86 puts with a delta of 1339. And again, in both cases, we're taking the closest options, which have a delta of no higher than 20 on both the call and the put side and selling those, hoping they both expire worthless. And then we're going out to the April expiration and buying the 98 calls, five points above the 93 shorts. And we're also buying the 81 puts five points below the 86 shorts. So again, as you can see, we have set up another double diagonal that we will be closing on March 21st. And again, using that exact same kind of calculation, you can see we collected a total of $250 this time, similar to the cash flow we got for the last double diagonal. In this case, our broker will require 4750 in capital. If we move to March 21st when the short options in this double diagonal expire, you'll see that TLT closed at 9070 that day. So again, just like last time, both the short calls up at 93 and the short puts at 86 both expired worthless because TLT's price expired between the strike prices of the short puts and the short calls, just like last time. And when we look at the surviving long calls up at 98 and the long puts down at 81, again they expire with a little bit of value with the 98 calls priced at 10 and the 81 puts priced at 2. So when we do that same kind of calculation, as you can see, we made a profit in this case of $370. And so at this point, we're going to start keeping a scorecard of how this year goes. So you can see that we entered the February and March trades. And now we're ready to move on to the April trade. Now with the April trade, we're going to be short the 94 calls and the 88 puts. And we'll be long the May 99 calls and 83 puts. And in this case, we collected $280 of positive cash flow at the outset of the trade for which your broker will require $4,720 in capital for the trade to be entered. Something interesting happened on this trade because if we move ahead to expiration on April 17th, you'll notice the TLT closed at 8753 which is actually below the puts that we sold at 80 at 88. And so when this happens, the correct protocol is to close that option before it expires so that you do not get assigned the TLT shares. So now in this case, one of our two options, namely the put option did not expire worthless. instead we had to buy it back first. You can see 47. So that's going to be a cost to us. But on the other hand, take a look at how much we'll receive when we sell those 88 puts that we own in May. Those are priced at 47 cents also, which is a much higher than usual price for those longs. So let's now analyze the result of the trade. As you can see, it's different than the prior two times for two reasons. You see, while the call expired worthless as usual, the puts cost us $470 to close. And while the long calls were still worth 60 when we sold them, the long puts had blown up in value and are now worth $470. And so even though we took a loss on our short put, we made a really nice profit on our longp put, resulting in the overall trade result being a positive $340 despite the fact that the TLT range was broken a little bit to the downside. So as you can see, this is a very forgiving trade with multiple ways to win. And so we'll add this trade to our scorecard as well. As it turns out, that April trade was the only trade during the 12-month period where one of the short options didn't expire worthless. And so, to avoid being repetitive, we're just going to show you the results of the rest of the trades. Although, you can check it for yourself if you have back testing software. And as you can see, after that April trade, in every subsequent month, TLT finished between the short calls and the short puts. So they went out worthless in each remaining case. While we got some extra profit from selling off those long calls and puts in the next month in each case, resulting in an annual profit of $3,645, which is a return of 77.5% for the 12-month period. And while this is not going to be the case in other years, there's definitely going to be years with losses. Nonetheless, the logic behind the strategy is strong, which is how we can get such an extraordinary return like we did in 2025. And so, what I'd like you to take away from today's video is that trading double diagonals can be a very strong trading practice as you gain experience if you follow the three important criteria we laid out for you in the beginning of the video, never skipping any of them. Now, the most challenging aspect will be to find a flat price chart like TLT. And if you can find a stock or ETF or index that's showing that kind of flat price pattern, the double diagonal can be a potentially rewarding trade for disciplined traders. Now, if you'd like to learn three more option strategies that our prot traders use, including the unique options trick that allows you to make money while you wait to buy stocks or ETFs at the price you want, and the options income strategy that allows you to make consistent money whether the market goes up or down or sideways, and how to make money on a stock or index trade, even if you're wrong on the direction, then click the link that's appearing ing right now at the top right hand corner of your screen. That will open up the free workshop registration page in a new window. So don't worry, you won't lose this video. Or you can register directly for free at optionsclass.com.

AayABdqDKIc.full_text: In this video, I'm going to be showing you an options trading strategy that has worked remarkably well over the last two years. It's a strategy that involves using Ballinger bands in conjunction with the RSI indicator. It's simple, easy to use, and can have an incredibly high success rate if you use it correctly. And the great part of this strategy is that involves just three really simple steps, which we're going to teach you if you stick around. So, let's not waste any time. Let's get right into it. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. The first step is to go into your online broker platform, whatever it may be, and set up a daily chart of the Q's, the ETF that represents the stocks comprising the NASDAQ 100 index. and pull up a daily chart of the cues and add Ballinger bands to the price chart just like we did here. Now, Ballinger bands are a technical analysis tool that consists of three lines, a middle simple moving average, the SMA. Two bands set at a certain standard deviation, most commonly two standard deviations above and below the simple moving average. And the idea behind the Ballinger bands is pretty simple. The two outside bands widen in times of high market volatility and shrink in times of low volatility in the markets to take into consideration the environment of the price movement. And taking into consideration that environment, it measures when stock's price has gotten extremely overbought or extremely oversold and therefore likely to reverse at that point. And so when a stock hits the top of the Ballinger bands, it's more likely to start selling off than rallying further. And when the stock or index gets to the bottom of the Ballinger bands, then the stocker index is likely to rally from that extremely oversold condition in both cases kind of magnetically drawn back to that simple moving average in the middle. Now, having said that, anyone who's worked with Ballinger bands knows that they are not by any means perfect. In fact, as you can see, if you examine this price chart closely, you'll see that there are times that the price will keep pushing up for a while after it's touched the upper band and conversely will sometimes keep pushing down for a while after it touches the lower band. But you'll also notice that that period usually doesn't last very long and then that reversion back to the simple moving average usually does happen. It just doesn't always happen immediately. And so what that means is that for example, if you see a stock or index hit the top of the Ballinger band and go short immediately, then you could definitely start to draw down and possibly hit your stop before that reversion occurs. And the same for the bottom band. You might go long a stock before it bounces and possibly get stopped out of the trade. And so we're going to be tackling this potential problem in two ways. And those two ways are in fact the remaining two steps of the strategy. So let's move on to step two which is to add the RSI indicator to the price chart. And as you can see there at the bottom there are two lines. The 30 line below the RSI indicator most of the time and the 70 line which is above the RSI indicator most of the time. And so when the RSI indicator drops below 30, that's considered an oversold condition, an extreme condition. And when it rises above 70, that is considered an extreme overbought scenario. Now, it's time to pair these two indicators together and start getting some profitable trades going. But don't stop there because in the third step, we're going to be adding an option strategy that hugely increases your chances of making money using these indicators. But that's the final step. And before I share that with you, I wanted to step back a minute and take stock of where we are. Because as you can see, we've suggested you combine two indicators together to make your trading decision. And the important thing to realize is that both of these indicators, the Ballinger bands and the RSI indicator are built around a concept called mean reversion. So what is mean reversion? Well, the word mean refers to a kind of an average. And the word reversion means to return. So to put it simply, the phrase mean reversion just means returning to the average, which is exactly what we're going to be shooting for with this strategy. So as an example, in most cases, if there's an extreme price movement in one direction, whether it's up or down, we can pretty much predict after that big price movement that the price to some extent will return back to the average and in particular the simple moving average in this case. So, if the price goes above the upper Ballinger band, we want to enter an options trade that makes money when the stock rolls over and starts selling off. And if the prices go below the bottom Ballinger band, we'd want to enter an options strategy that makes money when a stock rallies. But we're not going to stop there. Instead, we're going to require, which is a strict requirement, that the RSI is also above the 70 level to strengthen our conviction that the market has a good chance of rolling over soon before we enter the trade. And conversely, we won't enter a bullish options trade until the price crosses below the Ballinger band and the RSI indicator is at or below 30. And so, for example, here, even though the Ballinger band crossed above the upper band on March 21st, that's not good enough for us. We're going to wait until June 12th when the RSI indicator crosses above the 70 level before we would enter the bearish trade indicated by this combination when both indicators are suggesting a bearish trade. But we're not even done then because I promised you one more factor, one more step that will turn this into a very high probability trade. And that's the final step. And that step is to enter a 20 delta credit spread expiring a month later when both the RSI and the Ballinger bands are working in our favor. Now, what is a credit spread exactly? Well, there are two kinds of credit spreads actually. There's the call credit spread, which is a bearish options trade. And so you'd use that strategy when both the RSI and Ballinger bands are indicating a sell-off. And then there is the put credit spread, which is a bullish trade. And so you'd use that strategy when both indicators are suggesting a rally. So let's put this all together for you in a series of examples so that this whole thing becomes crystal clear for you. Let's head back to the first time this happened in 2024, which was on January 19th, 2024. And as you can see on that day, the Q's had closed at 42118. And you'll notice that the price had just pierced through the top of the Ballinger band and simultaneously the RS indicator exceeded 70. So, we have a green light to execute our bearish trade. Now you might have noticed that stocks will often hit the top of a ballinger band for that uh and the you know the RSI exceeds 70 but continues rising for a while as we mentioned earlier. So it's not wise for us to assume that the stock will automatically stop when either or both of these things happen. Therefore, we're going to give the market a little more room to rise after we get the green light, which will make it much more likely that we can win the trade. So, let's explain to you exactly how we're going to do this. So, let's first pull up an options chain that expires about a month later, which is that February 16th, 2024 options chain, which expires 28 days later. That's the closest we could get to an options chain that expires in 30 days on that particular date. And it's not usually going to be perfectly 1 month later, but get as close as you can. And then next, we want to look at the column, which is called delta. and we'll go to the closest call option to the 20 delta. And we pick the option which is a delta lower than 20, which is the 440 call, which has a delta of 17.51. And you'll notice, and this is really important, you'll notice that the strike price of that call is 440, which is almost 20 points above where the Q's are trading. So keep that in mind as we move along here. You're going to find that to be very important. And so at this point, we go ahead and sell 10 of those for $163. And at the same time, we're going to head up to the calls 10 points higher, the 450 calls, and we're going to go ahead and buy 10 of those for 53 cents, which is protection for our trade. Now, let's stop for a minute because I want to talk about the delta of an option. You see, what's called the delta of an option is a mathematically arrived at prediction of how much an options price is likely to move based upon how much the option stock itself moves. And while it's a bit of an involved topic, suffice it to say that an options delta has also been found to be highly correlated to the probability that an option will expire with value on the day that the option expires. So if we sell an option that has a delta of 20, then it means that the option mathematically has about a 20% chance of expiring with some value on the day it expires. And of course, a call option only has a value if the stock expires above that option strike price. So, if you think about it, what that also means is that the call option has about an 80% chance of expiring with no value, which in other words means that there's an 80% chance that the Q's will close below that 20 delta option on expiration day and that the option will expire worthless. Now, how this helps us is going to be clear in a minute and I think you're going to find the reason very interesting. Okay. So, when we sell a call option closer to the stock's trading price and buy a call option higher up on that same chain, when we do that, we're entering to what's traders referred to as a call credit spread. And so, in this case, we we've entered into a 440 450 call credit spread expiring on February 16th about a month later. Okay. So, now let's take a look at what has happened here cash flow-wise since we entered the trade. You see, when we sold those 10440 call options, we received the price of $163. But remember, each options contract represents 100 shares of stock. So, you multiply that by 100 and we sold 10 of them. So, when you multiply it all together, we receive $1630 in cash for selling those 450 calls. But then we turn around and buy the 450 calls for 53. So when you multiply all that out, we end up with an expenditure of $530. So when you net that out of the cash we receive for selling the $440 calls, we get a net amount of $1,100 of positive cash flow, which flows right into our account as soon as we make the trade. We're going to move to the day that this option expired on February 16th. And as you can see, the Q's actually closed at 430.57, which is actually up from where it was the day we started the trade when the Q's were actually down at 42118. Okay, so now that the options have expired, because this was expiration day, February 16th, let's take a look at how our options trade came out. You see, even though we were trading a bearish option strategy, the call credit spread, the fact is that the stock closed at 43057, up from where we entered. So, starting with the $1,100 in cash that we first received when we entered the trade. You'll notice that those 440 calls expired worthless. Why? Well, the Q's closed 9.43 points below those 440 calls. And remember, calls only have value unless the stock closes above that call strike price. So, it just expires worthless. As of course does the protective call up at 450 that we bought, which closed 19.43 points above where the Q's closed. And so, since both options expired worthless, our final trade profit is exactly the cash flow we received when we first entered the trade. And so, you might be asking this one simple question. And how the hell did we make money on a bearish trade when the stock went up? And that's the secret sauce to why this strategy works so well. You see, we located the call that we were selling so far above where the cues were trading when we entered the trade that it didn't matter that it rose modestly after we got our bearish signal. That's because both options were going to expire worthless. Even if the stock had risen all the way up to 440, they still both would have been worthless. So, as you can see, now we actually want the options to expire worthless because then we get to keep the cash flow that we first received. So, locating calls way above where the stock is trading, selling the call closer to the stock's trading price, and buying calls farther out from the stock's trading price, which we receive a credit for. Obviously, doing that will very often create a winning trade simply because we gave the market so much more room to run before it ultimately rolled over. Okay, so let's move on to the next case where we got a green light to enter a trade and that was actually a bullish trade on April 19th, 2024. You see, on that day, the market had been selling off and the Q's had on that day dropped to a closing price of 41465. And you'll notice that for a couple days before that, the bottom of the Ballinger band had actually been pierced by the price. However, it wasn't until April 19th that the RSI indicator also dropped to 30. So, that's going to be a bullish signal because both indicators are signaling a bullish trade. Now, remember, when we get bullish signals, then we're to place a put credit spread, which is the bullish strategy we're going to be using with this system. And so pulling up an options chain that expires again about a month later on May 17th, we go to the delta column, the put side now, and we find that the 390 put is 1655 deltas. So since that's right below 20 deltas, we'll go ahead and sell 10 of those. Buying 10 of the 380s further down the options chain by 10 points again for protection. And so again, we've entered a 20 delta put credit spread. this time, of course, on the put side because we're bullish. And by now, you've probably realized that we're located about 25 points below where the Q's are trading. And the reason for that is that this is a bullish signal. But to vastly improve the probability the trade is going to be profitable, we give it plenty of room this time to the downside because if the cues keep selling off for a while after we get the signal, we've got ourselves now a lot of additional room to still win the trade. And that's exactly how we won the last trade. This time it's just to the downside that we're getting the extra room. You see, when we entered this put credit spread, we again collected positive cash flow. As you can see from the calculation, we received 2610 for the 390 puts and we paid out$,580 for the 380 puts. And that resulted in $1,30 of positive cash flow. And so, how do puts expire worthless? Because that's obviously what we want here. We want to just keep the cash flow and have the options expire worthless. So, how do puts expire worthless? Well, that happens if the Q's expire on expiration date above the location of both the puts. So, locating them both far far below where the cues are trading gives us a much higher probability of both of them expiring worthless and just pocketing the cash flow we first received. You see, the location of the credit spreads at 20 deltas is the key to why this strategy has such a high probability of winning, particularly when we get multiple signals that the market is about to start rallying pretty soon, which is our requirement to make the trade. WXBxLHdYFi8.full_text: It's time to stop treating every setup like they're equal. Some trades give us rare windows where the checks in favor line up so perfectly that being conservative is actually our biggest risk. Like the recent parabolic blowoffs in gold and silver and even FSLY earnings today. In these trades, the edge isn't just present, it's overwhelming. There are other trades that have edge that we should be taking, but they're just normal trades. These trades are about quality control. The most undertalked about part of trading is the value of grading your setups correctly. In fact, I'd argue it's the whole game. Today, we're breaking down the mechanics of A+ trades and why your ability to recognize them is the difference between spinning your wheels and scaling your account. Welcome to the Trading Floor Podcast, episode 10. I'm Tim Beldin here with Garrett Dryen and our guy Kurt behind the scenes. So Garrett, why is grading setups important to you? >> All right. Well, first of all, like I think I think that it it is important to everybody whether they realize it or not. And then once you realize that, you can take it to the max, which is like kind of where it should be because everyone knows how to pass on a trade, >> right? And like you said, this is this is not talked about. Everyone talks about entries >> and maybe exits, right? What stocks to buy, what stocks to sell. >> We don't talk a lot about this trade is way better than this trade. I'm risking twice as much on this trade than I am on this trade, right? And you said it's the whole game now. Everyone knows how to pass on a trade. To me, that's one end of the spectrum. That's zero risk. Right. And then we have our A+ trades, A++ trades like you just described. They're the magnitude of these trades is off the charts. There's tons of edge there. It's a rare occurrence. It's a special situation where you want to be able to push it. And there are various ways to push it, which I'd love to get into, but those are the two ends of the spectrum. So, not taking the trade is actually a way to risk zero, right? And then you have the maximum amount that you're willing to risk, and that would be your A+ trade. But now there are all these trades in between, right? Like A, B, C, A minus, however you want to call it, however you want to categorize it for yourself. But that's where we like to kind of live and lay out the groundwork for all of these trades, all of these different risk levels, and then get really good at grading these setups because the risk that we put forth on these trades really decides the end of the month how we did. And I like how you think of it in terms of a sliding scale versus just taking a trade or not taking a trade because passing is just risking zero allocation. >> Absolutely. Like you can take every trade. You just take some with zero risk, right? It's easy. >> And we say all the time, you know, passing on a trade is a trade in itself. >> Yeah. 100%. So, I I actually want to I brought a slide that I'd love to show you, Tim, that you've seen it before, but um it's kind of it's something we've been doing for a while that that I tried to kind of put into I tried to I tried to visualize this the best I could, right? And this is something our team uses. This is a risk system that that we've come up with. Now, not everyone has to use the same risk system, and I would say everyone should be catered to themselves. The key here is that we have different grades, right? You can see there on the on the x- axis on the left, A plus, A, A minus, B, C. We've got different grades. And each of those trades really means a number of different things. And I I tried my best to sort of encapsulate that in this chart to kind of sum up a lot of the work that we've done in the past and that we've been doing recently in terms of defining these things because I think this is so important. At least I know it is for my training and it's something I'm I'm still working on all the time, right? Because like once you have edge, it's like all about this. This is the whole like you said, this is the whole game. This is what separates those huge months from like a pretty normal month, right? Um, and this is our main focus for the entire year. Like even going back to our last monthly review, this is what we are honing in on this year. >> Do you want to sit in on all of our daily and weekly in-house trader meetings? There's no bigger edge retail traders can get. Visit smbtradingfloor.com to learn more. >> A lot of people that I talk to risk the same amount on every trade. And look, there's nothing wrong with that. Especially, I think like when you're just cutting your teeth and you're getting into it and and you really are unable to separate these different grades out, like if you can't identify the difference, like there's no reason in altering your risk, right? But soon as you start to gain edge and you've got a playbook and you understand those different plays and the variables that go into them that makes one trade better than the other, like we really have to start thinking about what is our tier for this trade? What's our risk tier for this trade? And it's like, how do we do that? Well, like at a lot of a lot of prop firms, like we'll have like a daily stop, daily risk limit, right? It's the amount we're allowed to lose in a day. We might have a weekly risk limit, a monthly risk limit. And for a lot of people, they're trading based on maybe a percentage of their account. I would still like recommend having a risk limit because it does two things. It a gives us guard rails, right? So, if we're trading in an environment that we're either like not seeing well or maybe it's just a really unfavorable environment, we're not just pounding our head against the wall and drawing down and drawing down and drawing down. there's a way to kind of stop and say, "Okay, I hit my wrist for the day. Cool it. Clearly, we're not seeing things well. Let's come back tomorrow." Um, but the other thing is that it allows us to scale. It's actually an instrument for us to grow. And that's like more of sort of the the point today that that I want to make with this is like you can see at the bottom left of the chart, Tim say it just says max risk a thousand. So, this is just a hypothetical amount, but let's just say our max risk limit for each day is $1,000. Like, can't lose more than a thousand. Now, we have a benchmark where we can take every trade that we take and risk a certain percentage of that particular risk limit. This is just a particular process that we use with you know with our joint accounts and stuff like that. And that represents the percentage of our max risk that we will be allowed to get up to on one of these trades. And and if everything aligns and we actually get that entry where we can really push it, then that's really like the risk that we should be taking on these trades. And then within that, like throughout this this graph that I created, all the other columns are just trying to describe things about these trades that are really quick and easy and simple so that we can associate these trades with these grades. >> And that's a great point you're making because if you just increase your daily stop to 2K on your A+ trades, instead of taking 800, you're taking 1,600. And that that's something Dr. s told me like a while back like he kind of like I you know when I was talking to him when I was like starting out we were going through this and and kind of building this process like that's one of the things that he said to me that really stuck with me where it's like when you're growing and you're trading bigger and you're you're performing well so you want to step up to the next level like you really shouldn't be thinking differently because that's all that's all of a sudden changing what you're doing right right you got there for a certain reason you you really don't want to change anything. You just want to risk more. So his suggestion is keep it the same percentage of your max risk. Raise that max risk, but psychologically just think I'm I'm just risking 20% right now. Like the same 20% that I risked yesterday. It shouldn't feel any different. And that's it's just a little mental trick to to be able to scale up. >> And I think we might end up talking about this because I I've been wanting to talk about it. And it's the same percentage. And so, so I mean my my whole focus right now and and just so far this year like we all have yearly goals is is nailing these grades. Like that's the only thing I care about when I'm trading. And I think sometimes like for me, I know we've talked about this for a lot of people. It's really beneficial to keep your goals like super targeted and like very specific rather than having like 10 trading goals where you kind of achieve none of them because it's like too hard to focus on all of them at once. every every day I come in, I'm just like, "Okay, I just want to get the grading right. That's it. That's that's how I decide if I did well that day or not." It's the only thing I'm thinking about when I go to bed at night. That's what I'm thinking about. I wake up >> that's my DRC every day. >> Yeah. Yeah. Exactly. Because I mean that that to me this this really is like the curve. You know what I mean? like this is where like I know that if I can improve in this area like I can get to the next level and like you know we have improved in this area but I think there's there's a there's another level here um and this is why we've been focusing so much on it. >> Garrett I have a question because I'm not seeing any checks in favor or specific entry criteria in this. So you want to just take me through kind of what you are saying with the opportunity profile and gut feel columns? >> Yeah. So the reason I did that is because like first of all we all have different playbooks and like a lot of us will have most of us will have multiple playbooks right and every play has different criteria for what might constitute an A+ trade or B trade right variables that go into those could be volume could be the catalyst could be the market environment like all these different things these things that in the bionic trader meetings inside Access we break down and work on quantifying right so that we can actually get better at this Would you like to gain the biggest edge a retail trader can get? All of our daily and weekly in-house trader meetings are now available to you. Just head over to smbtrainingfloor.com to find out more. >> What we're doing here in this chart is trying to simplify it because this chart is playbook agnostic, right? So, we're not getting into specifics in terms of variables. We're trying to really talk about like how we're supposed to act when we see a trade like this. How are we supposed to feel? How often does it show up? How special is this trade? Because these are all like mental cues that can sometimes, in my opinion, like get us to the grade much quicker because you know how it is. Like you're trading a a play and it's not necessarily like a what do they call like paint by numbers or by the book or black and white. like black and white. Yeah. >> Um where like you have your variables that matter to the play that you know if all are present theoretically it would constitute an A+ setup but nothing is never ex nothing is exactly the same all the time. jWASPhyn3Po.full_text: I used to get so frustrated taking loss after loss trading catalysts after reading the headline or the earnings number thinking I knew what was going on. I would see tons of stocks make massive moves after earnings and it wouldn't be the stock I was in. It sucked. Then I learned what really moves these names. This is a process that I put hundreds of hours into figuring out. And in the end, there are five things I found really matter. Today we are going to reveal those five checks in favor for grading a catalyst play. And the fourth one you're going to want to pay attention to cuz this will make or break your success in this play. With these catalyst plays, we're looking for potential year making moves. The whole game is having the information to ignore the maybe setups so you can focus on the overwhelmingly clear ones. So how do we identify these crazy clear setups? We're going to pull back the curtain on our process and break down how to interpret a catalyst in detail, how to use pre-market scanners to hunt for institutional volume, and find these names before the market opens. Welcome to the Trading Floor podcast, episode 11. I'm Tim Beldin, joined as always by Garrett Dryen. So Garrett, you were the one last Thursday that found this trade. It was an earnings day one in Fastley and you found it in pre-market and alerted our team. So, what were you doing that morning? >> Yeah, so Fastley was not on our radar. It was not one of the earnings plays that we were like anticipating looking at. We weren't talking about it the night before, but I have this scanner that we used that I really love this thing. It's so simple and we call it the pre-market extended hours percent AVAL scanner. And it's something we share in Bionic Trader inside Access meetings. Um, I talk about this all the time because we use it and we actually found this scanner or this particular indicator variable that we developed from doing some of the quant work, right? We were testing an earnings model. Oh, yeah. Trying to figure out what actually mattered to these plays. And we found that >> the more volume as a percent of its daily average volume the stock was doing in the extended hours, right? So when the report comes out in the after hours or if it's a pre-market report, you're just looking at the pre-market, the more volume it was doing, the higher probability of trending and making a move on that day. And we actually found that if if any of them were doing like less than 20% of their average daily volume, it really wasn't worth looking at for the earnings. We saw >> for earnings plays, right? Because they tend to do a lot of volume. So this is all like calibrated to earnings plays. Would you like to gain the biggest edge a retail trader can get? All of our daily and weekly in-house trader meetings are now available to you. Just head over to smbtradingfloor.com to find out more. >> And so I was just looking at this scanner. It's earning season. Think of the earnings season. This is like how we find names that we might want to look into. The reason for it, the reason I love this so much is there are so many earnings reports to look through. like I can't go into depth on every single report that comes out, especially when there's like hundreds of names reporting all at the same time, right? So, this is just a brilliant way of narrowing down that focus and like finding the names that you might want to look deeper into. And so, sometimes we see 20%. Okay, that's cool. Like that's something definitely anything over 20 we'll like look at. Sometimes we see 50%. Sometimes we see 80%. 80%'s big huge, right? We rarely see that. >> Fastley was doing 200%. Like we haven't seen this in a very long time. And that's that's it. That's what put it put it on our radar. I hadn't looked into the report yet, but I put it right in the chat being like, "We got to look into this. What's going on here?" Um, and that kind of snowballed, right? Okay. So volume is always a big one on these catalyst plays, right? Because >> like like why is volume to you important? Like obviously we've studied this on the quant side and we see that it is. But a lot of times when we like to actually take something and run with it, we want to we want to sort of connect that to something that makes sense to us as traders. Like why does it make sense that something needs to be doing tons of volume in order to be valid on one of these plays? >> Yeah, it we found it's the best way. There are other ways to scan for earnings. Like you could just look at the gap percent and then what I found is you just look at the gap percent it doesn't tell you enough if you're not looking at the implied move but the whole reason why the extended hours aval percent aval scanner that we have works so great is we're trying to find stocks in play. So stocks in play are stocks that are doing abnormal volume in the regular trading hours. So we're really trying to get an idea of what stocks are the most in play. And we found there's a pretty good correlation of the stocks that do the most volume in after hours and pre-market that then become in play intraday. And that's the whole game. >> Yeah. And like the way I kind of read that is like we want to make sure there's interest. >> Yes. >> In this report like we like obviously institutions can't do all their business in the pre-market like the big ones, right? TpAPTwLMb44.full_text: So, the reason why a lot of you traders fail in your options trading is because you don't know when to correctly get into a trade and just as importantly when to exit a trade. You're leaving a huge amount of money on the table because you haven't been trained on how to maximize your profits on your options trades. Trading psychology and risk management is not going to save you. You need to know when to correctly get into an options trade and when to take your profits or you'll just continue to fail or at best have weak mediocre results. It's as simple as that. If you get into a trade too early or you get into a trade too late, both of these scenarios will cause your options trading to fail. And so, the purpose of today's video is to cure that problem once and for all. What we're going to be teaching you in today's video is so easy and such lowhanging fruit that it's going to immediately improve your returns with one simple tweak. So, let's get into it. Hi, I'm Seth Freyber and I'm the head trader of SMB Capitals Options Trading Desk here in Manhattan. And there's a basic mistake that a lot of options traders make that caused them to leave massive amounts of money on the table. And so I want to jump into an illustration of exactly how this mistake is made and the simple tweak you need to implement to substantially impu improve your trading results. Now before we get into the options trading technique that we'll be teaching you in today's video, if you're absolutely brand new to options trading and you don't know much about how options work, we've put together a video for you to understand options basics. And if you click on the video appearing on your screen right now, it will lay the groundwork for you to understand the option strategy we'll be sharing with you in this video. Then when you're finished, you can come back and watch the rest of this video to show you how this works. Let's take a look at a chart of the Q's the, you know, the ETF that represents the tech heavy NASDAQ 100 index on November 21st, 2025. And as you can see, we've overlaid Ballinger bands on this chart. And as you probably know, when you a stock is touching the lower Ballinger band, most traders consider that to be a bullish signal. And so, as you can see, on that day, the Q's price, which was 58810 on the open, was sitting right on that lower Ballinger band. And so, let's say that we decide to implement a bullish options trade that day. Now, one of those popular option strategies to trade a bullish signal is known as the put credit spread. And so let's say on that day we pulled up an options chain of the Q's expiring on December 4th, 13 days later. And we look down at the put side of the chart. And as you can see, the put with a strike price right at the market uh price, the 588 put was selling for 1267. And so we went ahead and sold 10 of those. And then we headed down five points lower to the 583 strike, which was selling for 1076. And we went ahead and bought 10 of those. Well, when you do that, selling a put higher up on an options chain and buying another put lower down on that same options chain, when you do that, you are entering into what options traders refer to as a put credit spread. And so, in this case, we sold a 5.88 583 put credit spread expiring on December 4th. Let's take a look at the cash flow implications of what we just done because that's the best way for you to understand how this trade works. You see, we sold those 588 puts at a price of 1267, but remember, put options represent 100 shares of stock. And so, you multiply that price by 100. And we sold 10 of them. So, when you multiply it all together, the total cash inflow from selling those 588 puts was 12,670. But then we spent most of that by purchasing 10 of those 583 puts, which as you can see using the same kind of calculation, cost us $10,670. And so netting it all down, you end up with $1,910 of positive cash flow for which your broker will be asking you to have at least $3,90 in your account at entry, which is also the trade's worst case scenario, loss. Let's move forward to the end of the day, 4 days later on November 25th. And as you can see, at the end of that day, the Q's had closed at 608.889, right in the middle of the two Ballinger bands that afternoon. So, the stock was rallying, but it had certainly not become what anyone would consider to be overbought at that point. We're still in a bullish mode. So, let's take a look at where we are on our options trade at this point, right at the close on November 25th. And as you can see, the price of the 588 puts that we sold have now dropped to a $164, way down. And the price of the 583 puts that we own have dropped to a$120. Now, I'd like you to think about something at this point. This trade expires in 9 days on December 4th. Today's November 25th. If the cubes keep rallying and by December 4th they remain well above 588, then both of those puts are going to expire worthless because puts only have value if they confer upon you the right to sell shares at a better price than they're trading for in the market. No one's going to sell their shares at below market. And so if the cues remain well above 588, both of those options are going to expire worthless. and that original $1,910 that you received, well, you're just going to keep those as your profit. That cash becomes your profit. But let's think about another path that we could take. And that is what if we close the trade right now and started a new one immediately. A new one that expires on that same day but is moved up so that it reflects the current price of the cues, not the price that it was at 4 days earlier when we first put the trade on. So let's play this out. And so the first step is to close the existing trade. So let's look at the math of how that would work. The way that you close an options trade is to simply reverse the original transaction, buying back the puts you sold and selling the puts you bought. So, let's start out by remembering that we collected $1910 at the outside of the trade. Then you buy back the puts you sold and you sell the puts you bought. So, in this case, we'd buy back the 5.88 puts for $164. So that would cost us $1640, but we also cash in the 5.83 puts it $120. So we receive in $1,200 for those. So when you net it all down, we end up with a profit of $1,470 on the trade. Now, think about it. If we had waited nine more days and the Q's stayed above 588 during that period of time, we would have collected the full $1910. But if we close it now, only four days in, we collect $1,470. Well, that's 76% of the profit we would have made by waiting nine more days. That's a pretty good chunk of change for 4 days. But we're not done yet because the second step in this process is to sell a new put credit spread expiring on the same day as the old one would have, which is December 4th. But this time, we're going to locate the put credit spread at the current price of the Q's, not at the price it was at four days ago from which it has bounced. And so with the Q's now trading 10 points higher at 60889, the trader selects that same options chain the December 4th. That doesn't change, but he simply moves up the options chain to 608 and sells 10 of those 608 put options at a price of 609, buying 10 of the 603 options, five points lower at a price of 441. And when we calculate the fresh cash flow that we're receiving from this new spread, you can see using that same kind of calculations we used before that in this case we collect $1680. A little bit less than we did before because we're now four days closer to expiration than we were when we first entered the original trade. So there's less time left. The spread is going to yield a lower premium. So, let's now move forward to the day that this second trade expires, which of course is December 4th, the same day the old one would have expired. And as you can see, the cues did continue to rally. And on the day this trade expired, they closed at 62294. And so when we look at the outcome of this second trade, it's pretty straightforward because obviously both of the puts expired worthless as the Q's expired at 62294 and the puts were located at 608 and 603, way below where the stock closed that day. And so given that there's no value to puts expiring below where the stock's trading, both options have zero value at the end of the trade. Meaning you get to just simply pocket the entirety of that $1680 in cash flow that we received when we first sold that second put spread. And so think about it. Let's consider the two outcomes on December 4th. On the one hand, had we simply let the original trade expire, uh the outcome would have been fine. We would have made a profit of 1910 over the 13-day period of the trade. But by closing the trade early when it had attained more than 76% of the maximum profit possible on the trade that 1910, we got 76% of it in four days. And so we've all already earned a lot of the profit from that first spread. So if we turn right around and enter a new trade expiring on the same day, if our thesis is correct and the stock continues to rally until it hits the top of the Ballinger band, then we can vastly increase our profit potentially on the trade by reusing our capital a second time during the course of the trade. This concept is called the velocity of capital and it's very useful to greatly improve your returns as an options trader. And so what I'd like you to take away from today's video is that professional options traders are always on the lookout for opportunities to improve their velocity of capital by closing trades early when they've attained a substantial portion of their maximum profit. which in the case of credit spreads is the amount of cash that you first receive in hopes that both options will expire worthless. If your profit is capped out at that original cash flow and your profit level mid trade is already a substantial portion of that maximum profit, then it makes all the sense in the world in most cases to close the trade and re-establish a new one. And you can see from this example just how powerful that practice is. These are the kinds of tactics used by professional options traders, which is why they can squeeze much more in the way of profits out of the market. Now, if you'd like to learn three more option strategies that our prot traders use, including the unique options trick that allows you to make money while you wait to buy stocks or ETFs at the price you want, and the options income strategy that allows you to make consistent money whether the market goes up or down or sideways, and how to make money on a stock or index trade, even if you're wrong on the direction, then click the link that's appearing ing right now at the top right hand corner of your screen. That will open up the free workshop registration page in a new window. So don't worry, you won't lose this video. Or you can register directly for free at optionsclass.com.

sh5h0GJzjNk.full_text: Every morning before the market opens, I used to spend about an hour doing the same thing.  Opening a stack of research emails and newsletters, trying to piece together what actually mattered for the trading day.  The information was good, but it was scattered, and by the time I'd fully synthesized it, the market was about to open.  So I built a system that does it for me.  Now, every weekday morning, before 8.30am, an AI workflow reads my market research emails, pulls out everything that matters.  Macro context, economic data, stocks in play, key themes, and generates a structured trading briefing, along with a full dash forward that I could scan in just a few minutes.  In this video, I'm going to show you exactly how it works and how you could build your own version, even if you have zero coding background.  I built this myself, and I'm certainly not a developer.  Let me show you what this actually looks like.  This is the daily market rundown the system generates every morning before the open.  At the top, there's a quick market snapshot.  This gives me the key macro setup for the day in just a few sentences.  For example, it highlights the current Fed stance, the most important economic data,  being released that morning in any major geopolitical developments that could influence markets.  Below that is the macro tone section.  This summarizes the most important developments driving the market so I can quickly understand the bigger picture heading into the trading day.  It also expands on the key overnight developments.  That includes things like geopolitical events, commodity moves, or changes in investor sentiment.  Next is the economic calendar.  This highlights the key economic data releases scheduled for the day.  For each report, it shows the time of the release, the type of event, the prior number, the current estimate,  and a label indicating the importance of the release.  This makes it easy to quickly identify which reports are most likely to create volatility  and exactly when they're scheduled to hit the market.
